parts.passageTitle: Cutty Sark: the fastest sailing ship of all time

passage.text: Cutty Sark: the fastest sailing ship of all time

passage.label: A
passage.text: More than a third of the world’s soil is endangered, according to a recent UN report. If we don’t slow the decline, all farmable soil could be gone in 60 years. Since soil grows 95% of our food, and sustains human life in other more surprising ways, that is a huge problem.

passage.label: B
passage.text: Peter Groffman, from the Cary Institute of Ecosystem Studies, points out that soil scientists have been warning about the degradation of the world’s soil for decades. At the same time, our understanding of its importance to humans has grown. A single gram of healthy soil might contain 100 million bacteria, as well as other microorganisms such as viruses and fungi, living amid decomposing plants and various minerals.

That means soils do not just grow our food, but are the source of nearly all our existing antibiotics, and could be our best hope in the fight against antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Soil is also an ally against climate change: as microorganisms within soil digest dead animals and plants, they lock in their carbon content, holding three times the amount of carbon as does the entire atmosphere. Soils also store water, preventing flood damage: in the UK, damage to buildings, roads and bridges from floods caused by soil degradation costs £233 million every year.

passage.label: C
passage.text: If the soil loses its ability to perform these functions, the human race could be in big trouble. The danger is not that the soil will disappear completely, but that the microorganisms that give it its special properties will be lost. And once this has happened, it may take the soil thousands of years to recover.

Agriculture is by far the biggest problem. In the wild, when plants grow they remove nutrients from the soil, but then when the plants die and decay these nutrients are returned directly to the soil. Humans tend not to return unused parts of harvested crops directly to the soil to enrich it, meaning that the soil gradually becomes less fertile. In the past we developed strategies to get around the problem, such as regularly varying the types of crops grown, or leaving fields uncultivated for a season.

passage.label: D
passage.text: But these practices became inconvenient as populations grew and agriculture had to be run on more commercial lines. A solution came in the early 20th century with the Haber-Bosch process for manufacturing ammonium nitrate. Farmers have been putting this synthetic fertiliser on their fields ever since.

But over the past few decades, it has become clear this wasn’t such a bright idea. Chemical fertilisers can release polluting nitrous oxide into the atmosphere and excess is often washed away with the rain, releasing nitrogen into rivers. More recently, we have found that indiscriminate use of fertilisers hurts the soil itself, turning it acidic and salty, and degrading the soil they are supposed to nourish.

passage.label: E
passage.text: One of the people looking for a solution to his problem is Pius Floris, who started out running a tree-care business in the Netherlands, and now advises some of the world’s top soil scientists. He came to realise that the best way to ensure his trees flourished was to take care of the soil, and has developed a cocktail of beneficial bacteria, fungi and humus* to do this. Researchers at the University of Valladolid recently used this cocktail on soils destroyed by years of fertiliser overuse. When they applied Floris’s mix to the desert-like test plots, a good crop of plants emerged that were not just healthy at the surface, but had roots strong enough to pierce dirt as hard as rock. The few plants that grew in the control plots, fed with traditional fertilisers, were small and weak

passage.label: F
passage.text: However, measures like this are not enough to solve the global soil degradation problem. To assess our options on a global scale we first need an accurate picture of what types of soil are out there, and the problems they face. That’s not easy. For one thing, there is no agreed international system for classifying soil. In an attempt to unify the different approaches, the UN has created the Global Soil Map project. Researchers from nine countries are working together to create a map linked to a database that can be fed measurements from field surveys, drone surveys, satellite imagery, lad analyses and so on to provide real-time data on the state of the soil. Within the next four years, they aim to have mapped soils worldwide to a depth of 100 metres, with the results freely accessible to all.

passage.label: G
passage.text: But this is only a first step. We need ways of presenting the problem that bring it home to governments and the wider public, says Pamela Chasek at the International Institute for Sustainable Development. ‘Most scientists don’t speak language that policy-makers can understand, and vice versa.’ Chasek and her colleagues have proposed a goal of ‘zero net land degradation’. Like the idea of carbon neutrality, it is an easily understood target that can help shape expectations and encourage action.

For soils on the brink, that may be too late. Several researchers are agitating for the immediate creation of protected zones for endangered soils. One difficulty here is defining what these areas should conserve: areas where the greatest soil diversity is present? Or areas of unspoilt soils that could act as a future benchmark of quality?
Whatever we do, if we want our soils to survive, we need to take action now.

questionGroups.instruction:
Do the following statements agree with the information given in the Reading Passage?
In boxes 1–8 on your answer sheet, write
TRUE  if the statement agrees with the information
FALSE  if the statement contradicts the information
NOT GIVEN  if there is no information on this

questions.prompt: Clippers were originally intended to be used as passenger ships.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
originally intended / passenger ships

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“The fastest commercial sailing vessels… built to transport goods around the world, although some also took passengers.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Bài nói clippers được xây để vận chuyển hàng hóa, chỉ có một số cũng chở khách.

Đề nói: ban đầu tạo ra để chở khách → sai.

⇒ Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: Cutty Sark was given the name of a character in a poem.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
name / character / poem

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Cutty Sark’s unusual name comes from the poem...”
“...wearing a ‘cutty sark’ – an old Scottish name for a short nightdress.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tên lấy từ cutty sark = váy ngủ ngắn, không phải tên nhân vật.

⇒ Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: The contract between John Willis and Scott & Linton favoured Willis.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
contract / Scott & Linton / favoured Willis

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“...ensured that the contract with them put him in a very strong position.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
“strong position” = có lợi cho Willis.

⇒ Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: John Willis wanted Cutty Sark to be the fastest tea clipper travelling between the UK and China.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
fastest / UK and China

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Cutty Sark was designed to make the journey more quickly than any other ship.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Mục tiêu là nhanh nhất tuyến Anh–Trung Quốc.

⇒ Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: Despite storm damage, Cutty Sark beat Thermopylae back to London.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
storm damage / beat Thermopylae

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Cutty Sark reached London a week after Thermopylae.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Đề nói thắng nhưng bài nói về sau 1 tuần.

⇒ Đáp án: FALSE

questions.prompt: The opening of the Suez Canal meant that steam ships could travel between Britain and China faster than clippers.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Suez Canal / faster

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Steam ships reduced the journey time... by approximately two months.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tàu hơi nước nhanh hơn nhờ dùng kênh đào Suez.

⇒ Đáp án: TRUE

questions.prompt: Steam ships sometimes used the ocean route to travel between London and China.
questions.explanation:
steam ships / ocean route

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
Bài chỉ nói tàu hơi nước có thể dùng kênh đào Suez.

Bước 3: Phân tích
Không có thông tin liệu đôi khi họ vẫn dùng đường biển hay không.

⇒ Đáp án: NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: Captain Woodget put Cutty Sark at risk of hitting an iceberg.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Woodget / iceberg

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“bringing her dangerously close to icebergs...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
“dangerously close” = đặt tàu vào nguy cơ va chạm băng trôi.

⇒ Đáp án: TRUE

questionGroups.instruction:
Complete the sentences below.
Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.

questions.prompt: After 1880, Cutty Sark carried ___________ as its main cargo during its most successful time.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
main cargo / most successful time

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“This marked a turnaround and the beginning of the most successful period in Cutty Sark’s working life, transporting wool from Australia to Britain.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Thời kỳ thành công nhất của tàu là chở wool.

⇒ Đáp án: wool

questions.prompt: As a captain and ___________, Woodget was very skilled.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Woodget / skilled

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Richard Woodget, was an excellent navigator...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Câu hỏi: “As a captain and ___”
→ trong bài: “excellent navigator”

⇒ Đáp án: navigator

questions.prompt: Ferreira went to Falmouth to repair damage that a ___________ had caused.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
damage / caused

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Badly damaged in a gale in 1922...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Damage được gây ra bởi một gale (gió bão mạnh).

⇒ Đáp án: gale

questions.prompt: Between 1923 and 1954, Cutty Sark was used for __________.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
1923–1954 / used

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Dowman used Cutty Sark as a training ship...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Dùng làm tàu huấn luyện.

⇒ Đáp án: training

questions.prompt: Cutty Sark has twice been damaged by ___________ in the 21st century.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
damaged / 21st century

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“The ship suffered from fire in 2007, and again... in 2014”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Tàu bị hư hại hai lần do hỏa hoạn.

⇒ Đáp án: fire

passage.text: Saving the soilMore than a third of the Earth’s top layer is at risk. Is there hope for our planet’s most precious resource?Section AMore than a third of the world's soil is endangered, according to a recent UN report. If we don't slow the decline, all farmable soil could be gone in 60 years. Since soil grows 95% of our food, and sustains human life in other more surprising ways, that is a huge problem.

Section BPeter Groffman, from the Cary Institute of Ecosystem Studies in New York, points out that soil scientists have been warning about the degradation of the world's soil for decades. A single gram of healthy soil might contain 100 million bacteria, as well as other microorganisms such as viruses and fungi, living amid decomposing plants and various minerals. That means soils do not just grow our food, but are the source of nearly all our existing antibiotics, and could be our best hope in the fight against antibiotic-resistant bacteria. Soil is also an ally against climate change: as microorganisms within soil digest dead animals and plants, they lock in their carbon content, holding three times the amount of carbon as does the entire atmosphere. Soils also store water, preventing flood damage: in the UK, damage to buildings, roads and bridges from floods caused by soil degradation costs £233 million every year.

Section CIf the soil loses its ability to perform these functions, the human race could be in big trouble. The danger is not that the soil will disappear completely, but that the microorganisms that give it its special properties will be lost. And once this has happened, it may take the soil thousands of years to recover. Agriculture is by far the biggest problem. In the wild, when plants grow they remove nutrients from the soil, but then when the plants die and decay these nutrients are returned directly to the soil. Humans tend not to return unused parts of harvested crops directly to the soil to enrich it, meaning that the soil gradually becomes less fertile. In the past we developed strategies to get around the problem, such as regularly varying the types of crops grown, or leaving fields uncultivated for a season.

Section DBut these practices became inconvenient as populations grew and agriculture had to be run on more commercial lines. A solution came in the early 20th century with the Haber-Bosch process for manufacturing ammonium nitrate. Farmers have been putting this synthetic fertiliser on their fields ever since. But over the past few decades, it has become clear this wasn't such a bright idea. Chemical fertilisers can release polluting nitrous oxide into the atmosphere and excess is often washed away with the rain, releasing nitrogen into rivers. More recently, we have found that indiscriminate use of fertilisers hurts the soil itself, turning it acidic and salty, and degrading the soil they are supposed to nourish.

Section EOne of the people looking for a solution to this problem is Pius Floris, who started out running a tree-care business in the Netherlands, and now advises some of the world's top soil scientists. He came to realise that the best way to ensure his trees flourished was to take care of the soil, and has developed a cocktail of beneficial bacteria, fungi and humus to do this. Researchers at the University of Valladolid in Spain recently used this cocktail on soils destroyed by years of fertiliser overuse. When they applied Floris's mix to the desert-like test plots, a good crop of plants emerged that were not just healthy at the surface, but had roots strong enough to pierce dirt as hard as rock. The few plants that grew in the control plots, fed with traditional fertilisers, were small and weak.

Section FHowever, measures like this are not enough to solve the global soil degradation problem. To assess our options on a global scale we first need an accurate picture of what types of soil are out there, and the problems they face. That's not easy. For one thing, there is no agreed international system for classifying soil. In an attempt to unify the different approaches, the UN has created the Global Soil Map project. Researchers from nine countries are working together to create a map linked to a database that can be fed measurements from field surveys, drone surveys, satellite imagery, lab analyses and so on to provide real-time data on the state of the soil. Within the next four years, they aim to have mapped soils worldwide to a depth of 100 metres.

Section GBut this is only a first step. We need ways of presenting the problem that bring it home to governments and the wider public, says Pamela Chasek at the International Institute for Sustainable Development, in Winnipeg, Canada. 'Most scientists don't speak language that policy-makers can understand, and vice versa.' Chasek and her colleagues have proposed a goal of 'zero net land degradation'. Like the idea of carbon neutrality, it is an easily understood target that can help shape expectations and encourage action. For soils on the brink, that may be too late. Several researchers are agitating for the immediate creation of protected zones for endangered soils. Whatever we do, if we want our soils to survive, we need to take action now.

Complete the summary below.
Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.

If these microorganisms are lost, soil may lose its special properties. The main factor contributing to soil degradation is the

questionGroups.notesTitle: Why soil degradation could be a disaster for humans

questions.prompt: Healthy soil contains a large variety of bacteria and other microorganisms, as well as plant remains and __________.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
plant remains

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“living amid decomposing plants and various minerals”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Plant remains = decomposing plants.

⇒ Đáp án: minerals

questions.prompt: It provides us with food and also with antibiotics, and its function in storing ___________ has a significant effect on the climate.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
storing / climate

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“holding three times the amount of carbon...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Đất lưu trữ carbon → ảnh hưởng khí hậu.

⇒ Đáp án: carbon

questions.prompt: In addition, it prevents damage to property and infrastructure because it holds __________.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
prevent damage

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Soils also store water, preventing flood damage”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Giữ nước → giảm thiệt hại lũ lụt.

⇒ Đáp án: water

questions.prompt: If these microorganisms are lost, soil may lose its special properties. The main factor contributing to soil degradation is the ___________ carried out by humans.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
main factor

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Agriculture is by far the biggest problem.”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Nông nghiệp là nguyên nhân lớn nhất.

⇒ Đáp án: agriculture

questionGroups.instruction:
Complete each sentence with the correct ending, A–F, below.
Write the correct letter in boxes 18–21 on your answer sheet.

features.name: may improve the number and quality of plants growing there.
may contain data from up to nine countries.
may not be put back into the soil.
may help governments to be more aware of soil-related issues.
may cause damage to different aspects of the environment.
may be better for use at a global level.

questions.prompt: Nutrients contained in the unused parts of harvested crops

options.label: A=may improve the number and quality of plants growing there.
B=may contain data from up to nine countries.
C=may not be put back into the soil.
D=may help governments to be more aware of soil-related issues.
E=may cause damage to different aspects of the environment.
F=may be better for use at a global level.

questions.explanation:
ước 1: Keyword
unused harvested crops

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“Humans tend not to return...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Không đưa chất dinh dưỡng trở lại đất.

⇒ Đáp án: C

questions.prompt: Synthetic fertilisers produced with the Haber-Bosch process
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
synthetic fertilisers

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“release polluting nitrous oxide...”
“turning soil acidic and salty”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Gây hại nhiều mặt môi trường.

⇒ Đáp án: E

questions.prompt: Addition of a mixture developed by Pius Floris to the soil
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Pius Floris mix

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“a good crop of plants emerged”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Giúp cây phát triển khỏe hơn.

⇒ Đáp án: A

questions.prompt: The idea of zero net soil degradation
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
zero net degradation

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“help shape expectations and encourage action”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Giúp chính phủ chú ý vấn đề đất.

⇒ Đáp án: D

questionGroups.instruction:
Reading Passage has sections A–G. Which section contains the following information?
Write the correct letter, A–G, in boxes 22–26 on your answer sheet.
NB You may use any letter more than once.

questions.prompt: a reference to one person's motivation for a soil-improvement project
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
motivation

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“best way to ensure his trees flourished...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Động lực nghiên cứu cải tạo đất.

⇒ Đáp án: E

questions.prompt: an explanation of how soil stayed healthy before the development of farming
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
soil stayed healthy

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“nutrients are returned directly...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Đất khỏe nhờ chất dinh dưỡng quay lại.

⇒ Đáp án: C

questions.prompt: examples of different ways of collecting information on soil degradation
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
collecting information

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“field surveys, drone surveys, satellite imagery...”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Các cách thu thập dữ liệu.

⇒ Đáp án: F

questions.prompt: a suggestion for a way of keeping some types of soil safe in the near future
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
keeping soil safe

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“creation of protected zones”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Đề xuất lập khu bảo vệ đất.

⇒ Đáp án: G

questions.prompt: a reason why it is difficult to provide an overview of soil degradation
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
difficult / overview

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn
“there is no agreed international system for classifying soil”

Bước 3: Phân tích
Không có hệ thống phân loại thống nhất.

⇒ Đáp án: F

passage.text: Book ReviewThe Happiness Industry: How the Government and Big Business Sold Us Well-BeingBy William Davies‘Happiness is the ultimate goal because it is self-evidently good. If we are asked why happiness matters we can give no further external reason. It just obviously does matter.’ This pronouncement by Richard Layard, an economist and advocate of ‘positive psychology’, summarises the beliefs of many people today. For Layard and others like him, it is obvious that the purpose of government is to promote a state of collective well-being. The only question is how to achieve it, and here positive psychology – a supposed science that not only identifies what makes people happy but also allows their happiness to be measured – can show the way. Equipped with this science, they say, governments can secure happiness in society in a way they never could in the past.

It is an astonishingly crude and simple-minded way of thinking, and for that very reason increasingly popular. Those who think in this way are oblivious to the vast philosophical literature in which the meaning and value of happiness have been explored and questioned, and write as if nothing of any importance had been thought on the subject until it came to their attention. It was the philosopher Jeremy Bentham (1748-1832) who was more than anyone else responsible for the development of this way of thinking. For Bentham it was obvious that the human good consists of pleasure and the absence of pain. The Greek philosopher Aristotle may have identified happiness with self-realisation in the 4th century BC, and thinkers throughout the ages may have struggled to reconcile the pursuit of happiness with other human values, but for Bentham all this was mere metaphysics or fiction. Without knowing anything much of him or the school of moral theory he established – since they are by education and intellectual conviction illiterate in the history of ideas – our advocates of positive psychology follow in his tracks in rejecting as outmoded and irrelevant pretty much the entirety of ethical reflection on human happiness to date.

But as William Davies notes in his recent book The Happiness Industry, the view that happiness is the only self-evident good is actually a way of limiting moral inquiry. One of the virtues of this rich, lucid and arresting book is that it places the current cult of happiness in a well-defined historical framework. Rightly, Davies his story with Bentham, noting that he was far more than a philosopher. Davies writes, ‘Bentham’s activities were those which we might now associate with a public sector management consultant’. In the 1790s, he wrote to the Home Office suggesting that the departments of government be linked together through a set of ‘conversation tubes’, and to the Bank of England with a design for a printing device that could produce unforgeable banknotes. He drew up plans for a ‘frigidarium’ to keep provisions such as meat, fish, fruit and vegetables fresh. His celebrated design for a prison to be known as a ‘Panopticon’, in which prisoners would be kept in solitary confinement while being visible at all times to the guards, was very nearly adopted. (Surprisingly, Davies does not discuss the fact that Bentham meant his Panopticon not just as a model prison but also as an instrument of control that could be applied to schools and factories.)

Bentham was also a pioneer of the ‘science of happiness’. If happiness is to be regarded as a science, it has to be measured, and Bentham suggested two ways in which this might be done. Viewing happiness as a complex of pleasurable sensations, he suggested that it might be quantified by measuring the human pulse rate. Alternatively, money could be used as the standard for quantification: if two different goods have the same price, it can be claimed that they produce the same quantity of pleasure in the consumer. Bentham was more attracted by the latter measure. By associating money so closely to inner experience, Davies writes, Bentham ‘set the stage for the entangling of psychological research and capitalism that would shape the business practices of the twentieth century’.

The Happiness Industry describes how the project of a science of happiness has become integral to capitalism. We learn much that is interesting about how economic problems are being redefined and treated as psychological maladies. In addition, Davies shows how the belief that inner of pleasure and displeasure can be objectively measured has informed management studies and advertising. The tendency of thinkers such as J. B. Watson, the founder of behaviourism*, was that human beings could be shaped, or manipulated, by policymakers and managers. Watson had no factual basis for his view of human action. When he became president of the American Psychological Association in 1915, he ‘had never even studied a single human being’: his research had been confined to experiments on white rats. Yet Watson’s reductive model is now widely applied, with ‘behaviour change’ becoming the goal of governments: in Britain, a ‘Behaviour Insights Team’ has been established by the government to study how people can be encouraged, at minimum cost to the public purse, to live in what are considered to be socially desirable ways.

Modern industrial societies appear to need the possibility of ever-increasing happiness to motivate them in their labours. But whatever its intellectual pedigree, the idea that governments should be responsible for promoting happiness is always a threat to human freedom.
‘behaviourism’: a branch of psychology which is concerned with observable behaviour

questions.prompt: What is the reviewer's attitude to advocates of positive psychology?

options.label: A. They are wrong to reject the ideas of Bentham.
B. They are over-influenced by their study of Bentham's theories.
C. They have a fresh new approach to ideas on human happiness.
D. They are ignorant about the ideas they should be considering.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
advocates of positive psychology

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"they are by education and intellectual conviction illiterate in the history of ideas"

Bước 3: Phân tích

"illiterate in the history of ideas" = thiếu hiểu biết về lịch sử tư tưởng → họ bỏ qua những tư tưởng quan trọng trước đó.

⇒ Đáp án: D (They are ignorant about the ideas they should be considering)

questions.prompt: The reviewer refers to the Greek philosopher Aristotle in order to suggest that happiness

options.label: A. may not be just pleasure and the absence of pain.
B. should not be the main goal of humans.
C. is not something that should be fought for.
D. is not just an abstract concept.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
Aristotle + happiness

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"Aristotle may have identified happiness with self-realisation..."

"Bentham: pleasure and absence of pain"

Bước 3: Phân tích

Aristotle xem hạnh phúc là tự hoàn thiện bản thân, không chỉ là niềm vui hay tránh đau khổ.

⇒ Đáp án: A

questions.prompt: According to Davies, Bentham's suggestion for linking the price of goods to happiness was significant because

options.label: A. it was the first successful way of assessing happiness.
B. it established a connection between work and psychology.
C. it was the first successful example of psychological research.
D. it involved consideration of the rights of consumers.

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
price + happiness significant

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"set the stage for the entangling of psychological research and capitalism"

Bước 3: Phân tích

entangling = liên kết → Bentham tạo sự kết nối giữa tâm lý học và kinh tế/kinh doanh.

⇒ Đáp án: B

questionGroups.instruction: Complete the summary using the list of phrases, A–G, below.

notePassage.text: Jeremy Bentham was active in other areas besides philosophy. In the 1790s he suggested a type of technology to improve
for different Government departments. He developed a new way of printing banknotes to increase
and also designed a method for the
of food. He also drew up plans for a prison which allowed the
of prisoners at al times, and believed the same design could be used for other institutions as well. When researching happiness, he investigated possibilities for its
,  and suggested some methods of doing this.

questions.prompt: Jeremy Bentham suggested a type of technology to improve ___________ for different Government departments.

options.label: A=measurement
B=security
C=implementation
D=profits
E=observation
F=communication
G=preservation

questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
government departments

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"linked together through a set of conversation tubes"

Bước 3: Phân tích

conversation tubes → giúp các bộ phận trao đổi.

⇒ communication

questions.prompt: He developed a new way of printing banknotes to increase __________.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
printing banknotes

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"produce unforgeable banknotes"

Bước 3: Phân tích

unforgeable = không thể làm giả → tăng tính bảo mật.

⇒ security

questions.prompt: He also designed a method for the ___________ of food.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
food

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"keep meat, fish, fruit and vegetables fresh"

Bước 3: Phân tích

giữ thực phẩm tươi → bảo quản.

⇒ preservation

questions.prompt: He drew up plans for a prison which allowed the ___________ of prisoners at all times.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
prisoners

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"visible at all times to guards"

Bước 3: Phân tích

visible at all times = luôn bị quan sát.

⇒ observation

questions.prompt: When researching happiness, he investigated possibilities for its __________.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
happiness + methods

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"If happiness is to be regarded as a science, it has to be measured"

Bước 3: Phân tích

measured = đo lường.

⇒ measurement

questionGroups.instruction:
Do the following statements agree with the claims of the writer in the Reading Passage?
In boxes 35–40 on your answer sheet, write
YES  if the statement agrees with the claims of the writer
NO  if the statement contradicts the claims of the writer
NOT GIVEN  if it is impossible to say what the writer thinks about this

questions.prompt: One strength of The Happiness Industry is its discussion of the relationship between psychology and economics.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
strength + psychology + economics

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"We learn much that is interesting..."

Bước 3: Phân tích

Tác giả khen sách giải thích sự liên hệ giữa tâm lý và kinh tế.

⇒ YES

questions.prompt: It is more difficult to measure some emotions than others.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
some emotions harder to measure

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

Không thấy so sánh giữa các cảm xúc.

⇒ NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: Watson's ideas on behaviourism were supported by research on humans he carried out before 1915.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
research on humans before 1915

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"had never even studied a single human being"

Bước 3: Phân tích

Chưa từng nghiên cứu con người.

⇒ NO

questions.prompt: Watson's ideas have been most influential on governments outside America.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
outside America

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

Chỉ nói Anh dùng Behaviour Insights Team.

⇒ NOT GIVEN

questions.prompt: The need for happiness is linked to industrialisation.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
industrial societies + happiness

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"Modern industrial societies appear to need..."

Bước 3: Phân tích

Tác giả liên hệ nhu cầu hạnh phúc với xã hội công nghiệp.

⇒ YES

questions.prompt: A main aim of government should be to increase the happiness of the population.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
government should increase happiness

Bước 2: Tìm đoạn

"always a threat to human freedom"

Bước 3: Phân tích

Tác giả phản đối việc chính phủ coi tăng hạnh phúc là mục tiêu chính.

⇒ NO
